questions.question: When a model wants to use a tool, what does it actually do?
options: It directly calls the external API and returns the result to the user
It sends back a structured request asking the host application to run the tool
It pauses the conversation and waits for the user to run the tool manually
It searches the internet for the answer and caches it for later
questions.explanation: The model cannot interact with the outside world on its own. Instead, it returns a structured tool_use block — essentially a polite, precise request — asking the host application to execute the tool. The host runs it, sends the result back, and the model continues.

questions.question: A stop_reason of "tool_use" means the model's response is ready to be shown to the user.
questions.explanation: A stop_reason of "tool_use" means the opposite — the model is NOT done yet. It is saying: "I need you to run this tool and send me the result before I can give a final answer." Only when the stop_reason is "end_turn" is the response ready for the user.

questions.question: What are the three main parts of a tool definition?
questions.explanation: A tool definition has a name (a short identifier), a description (plain-language explanation of what it does), and an input_schema (what inputs the tool needs and their types). The description is especially important because that is how the model decides when to use the tool.

questions.question: Why does the host application run tools instead of letting the model call APIs directly?
options: Because models are not fast enough to make network requests
Because API providers block requests from AI models
Because it keeps the host in control — it can validate inputs, handle errors, and refuse dangerous actions
Because tool results need to be encrypted before the model can read them
questions.explanation: Having the host execute tools is a deliberate safety and reliability design. The host decides which tools exist, validates every input, handles errors gracefully, and can refuse to run anything dangerous. The model suggests actions; the host decides whether to carry them out.
